title: Why Do I Repeat the Same Mistakes?

situation.content:
The relationship ended the same way the last one did. The project failed for the same reason the previous one failed. And standing in the wreckage, the thought isn't even surprise anymore — it's a tired recognition. I knew this was coming. I could describe the pattern perfectly. I just couldn't stop walking into it.

The cruelest part isn't the mistake. It's the clarity — seeing the loop from the inside and still not being able to step out of it.

story.content:
Steve Jobs was fired from Apple in 1985. The reasons were clear and widely discussed — he'd obsessed over perfection at the cost of shipping, he'd alienated his team with impossible demands, he'd ignored what the market actually wanted in favor of what he believed it should want. He was thirty years old, and the post-mortem was brutal and public.

He understood what had gone wrong. He could articulate it. In interviews during that period, he described his own mistakes with striking precision — the stubbornness, the refusal to compromise, the way he'd treated people.

Then he started NeXT.

And within two years, he was doing exactly the same things. The computer had to be a perfect cube — not approximately cubic, geometrically perfect — which made manufacturing nearly impossible. The case was magnesium, matte black, beautiful, and wildly expensive. He insisted on an optical drive when the market wanted a floppy. He burned through engineers, demanding revisions that delayed shipping by years.

The machine was gorgeous. Almost nobody bought it.

Here is what makes the story land: this wasn't ignorance. He'd diagnosed the pattern publicly, clearly, with full intellectual understanding. He knew the shape of the trap. And he walked directly into it again — because knowing the pattern and breaking the pattern are completely different muscles.

The knowing lives in the intellect. The repeating lives in the identity. Some part of him needed to be the person who made the perfect object, regardless of cost — and that need was deeper than his understanding of it. The loop wasn't a failure of insight. It was identity asserting itself over insight.

He only broke the cycle later, at Pixar — where he forced himself into the role of business owner, not product visionary. He deliberately removed himself from the creative decisions. It worked. Not because he'd gotten smarter, but because he'd finally changed his position in the pattern instead of just describing it.

contrast.content:
There's a concept in addiction recovery called "the geography cure" — the belief that moving to a new city will fix the problem. New job, new relationship, new apartment. Everything external changes. The loop continues.

Recovery programs identified decades ago what Jobs discovered at Pixar: insight doesn't break the pattern. Only changing your role in the system does. You can't think your way out of a loop you're feeling your way into. You have to change what you do at the moment the familiar pull arrives — not after you've already followed it.

principle.content:
Knowing the pattern and breaking the pattern are completely different things.

Repetition isn't a failure of understanding. It's identity choosing the familiar pain over the unfamiliar change — because the loop, however destructive, is at least known. Breaking it requires changing what you do, not what you know.

psychology.content:
The repetition compulsion (Freud, 1920) describes the tendency to re-enact painful patterns — not because they feel good, but because they feel recognizable. The nervous system is wired to prefer the known over the unknown, even when the known is harmful, because prediction feels safer than surprise.

Beneath it sits a simpler mechanism: identity-consistent behavior. If "I am the person who makes the perfect thing" is the deepest story, then situations that trigger that story will pull the same response — regardless of how many times the response has failed. The insight stays intellectual; the pull is felt in the body, pre-verbal, arriving before conscious thought can intervene.

The gap between knowing and doing is called the intention-action gap. Knowing the pattern creates an illusion of control — "I see it now, so I won't do it again" — but seeing a whirlpool and escaping one require entirely different abilities.

move.content:
1. Map the loop in writing. Not "I keep messing up" — the specific sequence. "First I feel X, then I do Y, then Z happens." The loop has a shape. Drawing it makes it an object outside you instead of a current pulling you along.

2. Identify the familiar pull. What does the moment before the mistake feel like in the body? Urgency? Righteousness? The need to prove something? That felt sense is the actual trigger — not the situation itself.

3. Change position, not just intention. Like Jobs at Pixar — don't just promise to behave differently in the same role. Change your role in the system. Delegate the thing you over-control. Remove yourself from the situation where the pull is strongest.

4. Build a pause at the choice point. Between the trigger and the old response, insert one physical act — walk around the block, sleep on it, text someone. You're not fixing the pattern. You're interrupting it long enough for a different response to become possible.

5. Expect the pull to feel wrong. Breaking a loop feels like self-betrayal — because it is. You're betraying the old identity. That discomfort is the signal you're actually doing something different, not a sign you're making a mistake.
